name: PostgreSQL - Indexing and Partitioning
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in PostgreSQL - Indexing and Partitioning as a competency should be able to explain the role and types of indexes (B-tree, Hash, GIN, GiST, BRIN), their structure, selectivity, and impact on query planning (sequential, index, index-only, bitmap scans), as well as the overhead on write performance and VACUUM. They should understand declarative partitioning (range, list, hash), partition keys, pruning, default partitions, and legacy inheritance-based partitioning, including limitations like lack of global indexes and foreign key behavior. Capable of designing and tuning indexes (multi-column, expression, partial, covering/INCLUDE), aligning with query patterns, avoiding redundancy, and managing fillfactor and bloat. Able to design partition strategies for data lifecycle, pruning, and workload (time-based, business, hash keys), plan partition hierarchies and granularity, and manage partition evolution (DETACH, ATTACH, DROP, split/merge). Skilled in analyzing query plans with EXPLAIN/ANALYZE, diagnosing index/partition usage, tuning queries for sargability, and using catalog views (pg_stat_all_indexes, pg_index, pg_partitioned_table) and extensions (pg_stat_statements, auto_explain, pg_partman). Can plan and execute index/partition maintenance (REINDEX, CLUSTER, pg_repack), coordinate VACUUM/autovacuum, manage index consistency across partitions, and perform schema changes safely. Understands transactional safety, WAL, locking behaviors, index/partition impact on backup and recovery, and edge cases like skewed distributions. Uses admin tools (psql, pgAdmin), migration tools (Liquibase, Flyway), and monitors index/partition health and performance. Collaborates with developers and DBAs, documents strategies, enforces naming and design standards, mentors juniors, and participates in schema governance and post-incident reviews.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core Indexing Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understand the role of indexes in PostgreSQL’s execution engine and how they impact read and write performance, memory, and storage
- Explain how PostgreSQL chooses between sequential scan, index scan, index-only scan, and bitmap index scan based on the query, statistics, and cost model
- Demonstrate clear understanding of all major PostgreSQL index types and where they apply:
  - B-tree indexes as the default general-purpose index for equality and range predicates
  - Hash indexes for equality predicates and their current reliability and WAL characteristics
  - GIN indexes for documents, arrays, full-text search, and containment operators
  - GiST indexes for geometric, range, and nearest-neighbor operations
  - BRIN indexes for very large, naturally ordered tables where block-range summaries are effective
- Understand the logical structure of B-tree indexes (pages, levels, root/branch/leaf nodes) and how that translates into lookup and update complexity
- Understand the concept of index selectivity and how predicate selectivity influences planner choices
- Understand the overhead of indexes on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE performance and on VACUUM/auto-VACUUM activity
- Recognize how NULL values, collations, and data types interact with index behavior and sort order
- Understand the difference between clustered and non-clustered physical ordering, and the implications of CLUSTER on an index
- Explain index-only scans: what visibility maps are, when index-only scans are possible, and why they may or may not be chosen","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Core Partitioning Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understand the goals of partitioning in PostgreSQL: performance (pruning), manageability (archival), and maintenance (vacuuming, index rebuilds, data lifecycle)
- Clearly differentiate between logical partitioning and physical sharding; understand what PostgreSQL native (declarative) partitioning does and does not provide
- Understand declarative partitioning concepts:
  - Partitioned tables and partition children
  - Partition keys and partition bounds
  - Range, list, and hash partitioning strategies and their behavioral differences
  - Default partitions and their role in capturing out-of-range data
- Understand how partition pruning works and what is required for it to be effective (constant or parameterized partition key predicates, stable expressions, planner capabilities)
- Explain how indexes interact with partitioning:
  - Local (per-partition) indexes and their implications
  - Index naming conventions across partitions
  - Distinction between indexes defined on the partitioned table versus indexes created on individual partitions
- Understand constraint-based partitioning (older inheritance-based approach) in principle, including its role in legacy systems and migration to declarative partitioning
- Understand limitations of PostgreSQL partitioning at this level:
  - No global indexes across partitions
  - Behavior of foreign keys involving partitioned tables
  - Impact on query planning complexity and plan caching
- Recognize when partitioning may hurt rather than help (too many partitions, poor partition key choice, uneven data distribution)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Index Design and Optimization","scope_included":"- Design appropriate index strategies for OLTP and mixed workloads based on query patterns and data characteristics
- Choose effective index keys given predicates, ORDER BY, JOIN conditions, and GROUP BY clauses
- Design and use multi-column indexes effectively:
  - Understand index column order and its impact on equality vs range predicates
  - Align index column ordering with typical WHERE and ORDER BY clauses
- Design and use expression indexes:
  - Create indexes on computed expressions (e.g., functional transforms, normalized text) to match common query predicates
  - Understand determinism and immutability requirements for index expressions
- Design and use partial indexes:
  - Define predicates for partial indexes to target hot subsets (e.g., active rows, recent dates, non-null values)
  - Ensure that query predicates are compatible with partial index predicates for index usage
- Use covering indexes (INCLUDE columns) to reduce heap access and enable index-only scans where beneficial
- Tune index storage parameters at an intermediate level:
  - Understand fillfactor and how to adjust it for write-heavy or update-heavy workloads
  - Recognize impact of page-level storage decisions on bloat and fragmentation
- Evaluate trade-offs between multiple specialized indexes vs fewer general-purpose indexes given memory and maintenance costs
- Avoid redundant or overlapping indexes by understanding when two indexes provide equivalent or near-equivalent support to the planner
- Design composite strategies combining B-tree with GIN/GiST/BRIN for mixed access patterns and document-like schemas, while understanding their update and storage costs
- Align indexing strategy with application-level access patterns, including ORMs and report queries, and actively challenge inefficient patterns when needed","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Partitioning Design and Strategy","scope_included":"- Select appropriate partition keys based on query patterns, data lifecycle, and data distribution:
  - Time-based keys for time-series or log-like data
  - Business keys for tenant-based isolation or regulatory requirements
  - Hash-based keys for balancing data volume across partitions
- Select appropriate partitioning method for a given workload and dataset size (range, list, hash, or hybrid layered approaches)
- Design partition hierarchies:
  - Determine reasonable number of partitions to balance pruning benefits vs planning/management overhead
  - Define partition granularity (e.g., daily vs monthly) based on data volume and retention policies
- Plan for data lifecycle management:
  - Use partitioning for efficient archival (DETACH, ATTACH, and DROP partitions instead of row-by-row deletes)
  - Plan for cold storage or externalization of old partitions (e.g., via foreign tables)
- Design partitioned tables so common queries fully benefit from partition pruning:
  - Ensure partition key appears in WHERE clauses and joins
  - Avoid non-sargable expressions on partition keys that block pruning
- Understand and design for limitations:
  - Plan for lack of global indexes by replicating critical indexes per partition
  - Recognize planner overhead for very large numbers of partitions and design around it
- Consider partitioning interactions with constraints and foreign keys:
  - Use primary keys and unique constraints in a partitioned context where possible
  - Understand how foreign keys reference partitioned parents and the implications for insert performance and constraint checks
- Plan and design for partition evolution:
  - Add new partitions ahead of time for time-based data
  - Plan split/merge strategies logically, even if implemented via detach/attach and data moves
  - Anticipate rebalancing needs when distributions change over time","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Query Planning, Index/Partition Usage, and Performance Tuning","scope_included":"- Analyze query plans using EXPLAIN and EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to understand:
  - Whether indexes are being used as intended
  - Whether partition pruning is occurring
  - The relative cost of index scans vs sequential scans vs bitmap scans
- Diagnose why a query is not using an index and determine corrective actions:
  - Examine predicates for non-sargable expressions or type mismatches
  - Check statistics (ndistinct, correlation, histogram) and update them via ANALYZE
  - Evaluate misconfigured cost settings only at a basic-to-intermediate level
- Identify when partitioning is or is not being leveraged:
  - Detect lack of partition pruning in plans and refactor queries to enable it
  - Recognize when partitioning overhead outstrips its pruning benefits
- Tune queries to better leverage indexes and partitions:
  - Rewrite predicates to align with index keys and partition keys
  - Push filters down into subqueries or CTEs where appropriate
  - Reduce use of functions or casts on indexed columns in WHERE/JOIN conditions
- Evaluate and adjust index designs given observed plans and runtime metrics:
  - Add or modify indexes to satisfy frequent sort/group/join operations
  - Remove or consolidate indexes that are never or rarely used
- Use system catalog and statistics views related to indexes and partitions at an intermediate level:
  - pg_stat_all_indexes / pg_stat_user_indexes to inspect usage and scan counts
  - pg_index, pg_class, pg_partitioned_table, pg_inherits to understand table/index/partition relationships
- Use auto_explain or query logging (where available) to capture slow queries and understand their index/partition usage patterns
- Perform basic, safe runtime experiments (for learning and diagnostics) using planner enable/disable switches (e.g., enable_seqscan) without relying on them as permanent fixes","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Index and Partition Maintenance and Operations","scope_included":"- Plan and execute index maintenance:
  - Use REINDEX and REINDEX CONCURRENTLY appropriately to rebuild bloated or corrupted indexes with minimal downtime
  - Recognize when CLUSTER or pg_repack-like tools may be appropriate to improve locality and reduce bloat (at the level of understanding and planning, if tools are available in the environment)
- Detect and address index bloat:
  - Use statistics and size views to detect bloat (e.g., comparing index size vs table size, tuple counts vs page counts)
  - Understand how heavy update/delete workloads and HOT updates contribute to bloat
- Coordinate VACUUM and auto-VACUUM behavior with index and partitioned workloads:
  - Understand how VACUUM maintains indexes and visibility maps
  - Recognize when auto-VACUUM configuration may need adjustment due to partitioning or indexing choices
- Maintain partition sets operationally:
  - Create new partitions on schedule (especially for time-based partitions)
  - DETACH and DROP old partitions to enforce data retention
  - ATTACH existing tables as partitions while ensuring constraint and data consistency
- Manage indexes within partition hierarchies:
  - Ensure newly created partitions receive required indexes (via parent definition or explicit creation)
  - Maintain consistent index definitions across partitions for predictable query planning
- Execute schema changes involving partitioning and indexing with controlled risk:
  - Add/remove indexes on large partitioned tables using CONCURRENTLY where appropriate
  - Convert non-partitioned tables to partitioned tables via create-copy-attach patterns or logical reorganization
- Plan and execute maintenance operations to minimize production impact:
  - Schedule heavy operations in low-traffic windows
  - Monitor blocking and locking behavior arising from index/partition changes and resolve safely","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Reliability, Safety, and Edge Cases in Indexing and Partitioning","scope_included":"- Understand the transactional and crash-safety properties of indexes:
  - WAL logging implications for different index types
  - Behavior of concurrent index creation and its safety guarantees
- Handle index-related failures and anomalies safely:
  - Diagnose and resolve invalid or failed indexes (e.g., following crashes or aborted operations)
  - Recognize when an index is marked invalid and understand how PostgreSQL treats such indexes
- Understand locking behavior related to index and partition operations:
  - Lock levels acquired for CREATE INDEX (normal vs CONCURRENTLY), REINDEX, ATTACH/DETACH, and partition DDL
  - How these locks interact with concurrent reads and writes
- Safely work with large tables and partitions:
  - Understand how extremely large indexes and many partitions influence backup size, restore time, and recovery
  - Anticipate autovacuum and analyze behavior on large partitioned tables and their impact on index usability
- Recognize and manage non-obvious behaviors:
  - Impact of heavily skewed data distributions on index effectiveness and partition balance
  - Limitations of constraints and foreign keys with partitioned tables (e.g., performance trade-offs, cascading behavior)
- Ensure that backup and recovery strategies account for partitioned schemas and large indexes:
  - Understand that logical backup tools and physical backups treat partitions and indexes differently but consistently
  - Consider how frequent schema changes to partitions and indexes interact with backup windows and recovery scenarios","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling, Extensions, and Environment Setup (Indexing & Partitioning Focus)","scope_included":"- Use common PostgreSQL client and admin tools to design, inspect, and maintain indexes and partitions:
  - psql for DDL, EXPLAIN, and catalog/statistics inspection
  - Graphical tools (such as pgAdmin or DBeaver) to visualize tables, indexes, and partition structures, where available
- Use relevant PostgreSQL extensions and modules related to indexing and partitioning at an intermediate level:
  - pg_stat_statements to identify high-cost queries and their dependence on indexes and partitions
  - auto_explain to log expensive queries and inspect actual index/partition usage
  - Partition-management helpers (such as pg_partman or equivalent internal tooling) where present, understanding their configuration and operational patterns
- Use monitoring and observability tooling with an indexing/partitioning perspective:
  - Track index usage patterns and bloat indicators
  - Track partition growth, row counts, and storage consumption over time
  - Watch query latency and plan changes for partitioned tables after schema/index modifications
- Work effectively with environment configuration that influences indexing and partitioning:
  - Understand the impact of work_mem, maintenance_work_mem, effective_cache_size, and autovacuum settings on index operations and partition maintenance at a practical level
  - Participate in discussions around tuning these settings to support heavy indexing and large partitioned tables, without necessarily being the ultimate authority on global database tuning
- Use migration and schema management tools (e.g., Liquibase, Flyway, Sqitch, or in-house equivalents) to manage index and partition changes in a controlled, versioned manner
- Employ safe deployment practices for index and partition DDL:
  - Break large operations into smaller steps where possible
  - Use feature flags or phased rollouts when adding indexes intended to support new queries or application paths","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Governance, and Documentation","scope_included":"- Collaborate with application developers to design queries and schemas that make effective use of indexes and partitioning:
  - Review query changes for their impact on existing indexes and partitions
  - Propose index and partition designs early in feature development
- Communicate performance implications of indexing and partitioning decisions to non-database specialists in clear, actionable terms
- Document indexing and partitioning strategies for critical tables:
  - Record the purpose of each significant index and its target query patterns
  - Document partitioning schemes, keys, and lifecycle policies for major partitioned tables
- Participate in schema governance and review processes:
  - Enforce team standards for index naming, partition naming, and consistent design patterns
  - Ensure that new features do not introduce redundant or conflicting indexes or unsustainable partition strategies
- Mentor junior engineers on foundational indexing and partitioning concepts, including how to read query plans and when to propose partitioning solutions
- Coordinate with operations/DBA teams:
  - Align maintenance windows for index rebuilds and partition maintenance with operational constraints
  - Provide input into capacity planning for storage and I/O with regard to large indexes and partitions
- Contribute to post-incident reviews where performance or outages involved index or partition issues, helping to identify root causes and preventive practices","is_non_core_section":true}]}
